content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
• Sacramento, CA 958 12-0807
(916)322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
April 8, 1998
Daniel Wentland
Wentland Construction Co.
1913 Dean Road
Paradise, CA 95969
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-98-050
Dear Mr. Wentland:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest
disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 Because your inquiry is general
in nature, we will treat your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c).
2 Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as evaluation of
any conduct which may already have taken place.
QUESTION
What is the definition of “gross income” for reporting purposes on the Statement of
Economic Interests, Form 700?
CONCLUSION
Gross income is the total amount of income received before deducting expenses, losses,
or taxes.
Government Code sections 81000- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


File No. 1-98-050
Page Two
ANALYSIS
The Act requires public officials to disclose sources of income that are located or doing
business in their jurisdiction. Section 82030 defines income, in part, as:
[A] payment received, including but not limited to any salary, wage,
advance .... Income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income
of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater.”
The Commission has previously advised that Section 82030 refers to gross, rather than
net, income. There are several policy reasons behind the Commission’s interpretation of Section
82030. First, gross receipts are easily and objectively determinable, whereas the amount ofnet
income may vary with the accounting methods used and the tax deductions taken by a filer.
Second, in situations where the business entity suffers a net loss, a “net receipts” test would
allow a filer to not disclose substantial sources of income to the business. (Kennedy Advice
Letter, No. A-82-065.)
In your letter you stated you are a building contractor and you asked if gross income is
defined under the Act the same as state and federal standards, which is gross receipts less “hard”
costs.
You gave an example ofbuilding a $100,000 home and asked if the concrete, lumber,
shingles, etc., and all subcontractors could be subtracted from $100,000 to arrive at the amount
of the gross income. Using your example, under the Act, the amount of gross income received
would be $100,000. The concrete, lumber, shingles, etc. and all subcontractors would not be
subtracted. Therefore, you must disclose gross income you receive from your contracting
business of $250 or more. In addition, if you have a 10 percent or greater ownership interest in
the business, you are required to disclose the names of any persons that are sources of income to
the business in which your pro rata share is $10,000 or more. (Section 87207.)
If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By: Dixie Howard
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

Page Two
